schools.summary: The image drawn from the heroes of faith (ch. 11): 'wherefore seeing we also are compassed about (surrounded, as in an amphitheatre) with so great a cloud (nephos — a vast multitude) of witnesses (martyron — either spectators watching the race, or those who by their lives bore witness to faith), let us lay aside every weight (onkon — every encumbrance, anything that hinders, as a runner strips off all bulk), and the sin which doth so easily beset us (euperistaton — the sin that clings closely, easily entangles), and let us run with patience (hypomone — endurance) the race (agona — contest) that is set before us' — the believer, surrounded by the great host of the faithful, to cast off every hindrance and the clinging sin and run the appointed course with patient endurance to the end (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry).

themes: compassed about with so great a cloud of witnesses
let us lay aside every weight, and the sin which doth so easily beset us
let us run with patience the race that is set before us
the call to run the race

schools.summary: The runner's focus: 'looking (aphorontes — looking away from all else, fixing the eyes) unto Jesus the author (archegon — leader, originator, pioneer) and finisher (teleioten — perfecter, completer) of our faith' — keeping the eye fixed on Jesus, who both originates and perfects faith, the supreme example and goal; 'who for the joy that was set before him (the future glory and the redemption of his people — anti taken as 'in view of' the joy, or by some 'in exchange for'/'instead of' the joy he had) endured the cross, despising (disregarding, counting as nothing) the shame, and is set down (enthroned) at the right hand of the throne of God' — Jesus bearing the cross and scorning its shame for the joy to come, his endurance issuing in his exaltation, the crown that followed the cross (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

Cyril: Jesus, having endured the cross and despised its shame, is now seated at the right hand of God's throne — the exaltation that crowned his endurance, the encouragement that the believer's race, run looking unto him, ends in glory.

themes: Looking unto Jesus the author and finisher of our faith
who for the joy that was set before him endured the cross, despising the shame
the runner looking to Jesus
endurance crowned with glory

schools.summary: The exhortation to ponder Christ: 'for consider (analogisasthe — reckon up, compare, weigh carefully) him that endured such contradiction (antilogian — opposition, hostility, gainsaying) of sinners against himself, lest ye be wearied (grow weary) and faint (ekluomenoi — be loosened, exhausted) in your minds (souls)' — weighing how much Christ endured from the bitter hostility, insults, and opposition of sinful men (far greater than the readers had yet suffered), the antidote to spiritual weariness and discouragement being to consider Christ's far greater endurance, comparing our trials with his to keep us from fainting (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry).

themes: consider him that endured such contradiction of sinners against himself
lest ye be wearied and faint in your minds
the call to consider Christ's endurance
the antidote to discouragement

schools.summary: The gentle reproof: 'ye have not yet resisted unto blood (to the shedding of blood, to death), striving (antagonizomenoi — contending, as in a contest) against sin' — in their struggle against sin and persecution the readers not yet called to die, their suffering (real as it was) not yet reaching martyrdom; a real contest against temptation and the sinful opposition of persecutors, but not yet the bloody one, the reminder that they had not suffered the worst meant to shame them out of fainting (Barnes, Robertson, Henry).

Chrysostom: unlike Christ, who resisted unto blood and shed his own, and unlike the martyrs of old, the readers had not yet poured out their blood; their trials, though grievous, fell short of the supreme cost, and so gave no ground for discouragement.

themes: Ye have not yet resisted unto blood, striving against sin
the readers had not yet resisted to the point of blood
their suffering short of martyrdom
the contrast with Christ and the martyrs

schools.summary: The citation (Proverbs 3:11-12): 'and ye have forgotten the exhortation (paraklesis — the encouraging word) which speaketh (reasons, addresses) unto you as unto children (sons), My son, despise not thou the chastening (paideia — discipline, training, correction) of the Lord, nor faint when thou art rebuked of him' — the readers having forgotten a Scripture that addresses them tenderly as God's sons, warning against two opposite dangers: 'despise not' (do not make light of, undervalue, or scorn God's discipline as a small thing or a sign of mere displeasure) and 'nor faint' (do not lose heart or sink under discipline in despair) — the believer to take discipline rightly, avoiding both extremes (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry).

themes: the exhortation which speaketh unto you as unto children
My son, despise not thou the chastening of the Lord, nor faint when thou art rebuked of him
the exhortation not to despise the Lord's chastening
avoiding both despising and fainting

schools.summary: The ground of discipline (Proverbs 3:12): 'for whom the Lord loveth he chasteneth (disciplines), and scourgeth (mastigoi — flogs, applies sharper correction) every son whom he receiveth (accepts as a son)' — God's chastening the proof, not the contradiction, of his love; he disciplines those he loves as his sons, so that chastening is a mark of his love and of true sonship (far from showing rejection, it shows he treats the sufferer as a beloved child, the rod a sign of fatherly care); 'scourgeth every son' — no true son exempt, all whom God receives he disciplines, even severe correction flowing from love (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry).

themes: whom the Lord loveth he chasteneth, and scourgeth every son whom he receiveth
the Lord chastens those he loves
chastening the token of love and sonship
no true son exempt

schools.summary: The inference: 'if ye endure chastening (bear it patiently), God dealeth with (treats, offers himself to) you as with sons; for what son is he whom the father chasteneth not?' — the endurance of discipline evidence that God treats the sufferer as a son, chastening being part of the Father's dealing with his children and the mark of the family relationship (God's correction placing the believer among his children, not his enemies); every true father disciplines his children, an undisciplined son being unheard of, so God's discipline is the common lot of all his true sons, proving their sonship (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry).

themes: If ye endure chastening, God dealeth with you as with sons
for what son is he whom the father chasteneth not
chastening proves God deals with us as sons
discipline the mark of the family relationship

schools.summary: The reverse argument: 'but if ye be without chastisement (discipline), whereof all (all true sons) are partakers, then are ye bastards (nothoi — illegitimate children), and not sons' — to be without discipline would prove one no true son but illegitimate, for all genuine sons share in the Father's correction (the common experience of the whole family of God, none excepted); the undisciplined being unowned by the father, left without the correction that belongs to true sonship, so the believer should welcome chastening as proof he is a true son (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry).

themes: if ye be without chastisement, whereof all are partakers, then are ye bastards, and not sons
the lack of chastisement would prove illegitimacy
discipline the badge of true sonship
all true sons share in correction

schools.summary: The comparison (a fortiori): 'furthermore we have had fathers of our flesh (earthly, human fathers) which corrected (disciplined) us, and we gave them reverence (respect, submission): shall we not much rather be in subjection unto the Father of spirits, and live?' — if we submitted to and respected our human fathers who disciplined us (the lesser, familiar case), how much more should we submit to God, 'the Father of spirits' (the source of our spiritual being, or of all spirits, contrasted with 'fathers of our flesh' — God the Father of our higher, spiritual nature); and submission to his discipline issues in life ('and live') — true, spiritual, eternal life (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: we have had fathers of our flesh which corrected us, and we gave them reverence
shall we not much rather be in subjection unto the Father of spirits, and live
the argument from earthly fathers to the Father of spirits
submission issuing in life

schools.summary: The contrast: 'for they verily for a few days (during childhood, briefly) chastened us after their own pleasure (as seemed good to them, sometimes arbitrarily or by fallible human judgment); but he (God) for our profit (to benefit us, for our good), that we might be partakers (sharers) of his holiness' — human discipline brief and according to fallible judgment, but God's discipline never arbitrary, always aimed at our true advantage, perfectly wise and purposeful; the goal of God's chastening being our sanctification, that we may share in his own holiness, the highest possible end, making affliction a means of grace (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

Cyril: God disciplines that we might partake of his holiness — the high aim of all his chastening being our sanctification, that we may share in his own holy nature; so affliction, rightly received, becomes the means by which God conforms us to himself.

themes: they verily for a few days chastened us after their own pleasure; but he for our profit
that we might be partakers of his holiness
God's discipline aimed at our partaking of his holiness
the holy aim of chastening

schools.summary: The honest acknowledgment: 'now no chastening for the present (at the time) seemeth to be joyous, but grievous (painful, sorrowful)' — discipline not pleasant while it lasts, felt as pain not joy, the writer not pretending otherwise; 'nevertheless afterward (later, when it has done its work) it yieldeth (gives back, produces) the peaceable fruit of righteousness (the fruit which is righteousness, bringing peace) unto them which are exercised (gegymnasmenois — trained, as an athlete by exercise) thereby' — though painful at the time, discipline afterward bearing a harvest of righteousness and peace, the bitter root yielding sweet fruit, but only to those trained by it who submit and let it do its work (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Chrysostom).

themes: no chastening for the present seemeth to be joyous, but grievous
afterward it yieldeth the peaceable fruit of righteousness unto them which are exercised thereby
the painful discipline yielding the peaceable fruit of righteousness
trained by discipline as an athlete

schools.summary: The figure (Isaiah 35:3): 'wherefore lift up (strengthen, raise) the hands which hang down (drooping, slack with discouragement), and the feeble (paralyzed, weakened) knees' — the picture of an exhausted, discouraged runner or traveler, the readers to rouse themselves from spiritual fatigue; 'hands which hang down' picturing flagging zeal and discouragement, 'the feeble knees' weakened by fear or fatigue — the call to renewed effort and fresh courage after the teaching on discipline, to mutual encouragement and personal resolve in the race (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry).

themes: lift up the hands which hang down, and the feeble knees
the call to renew strength
rousing the weary to fresh effort
fresh courage after the teaching on discipline

schools.summary: The figure (Proverbs 4:26): 'and make straight (orthas — level, smooth) paths for your feet, lest that which is lame (the weak, halting believer) be turned out of the way (dislocated, or led astray); but let it rather be healed (restored, strengthened)' — making the way plain and even, removing obstacles both for oneself and for weaker believers, lest a rough path cause the lame to stumble and wander and be driven off; the goal being not to lose the weak but to heal and restore them, the strong smoothing the way so that the lame are helped toward recovery (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry).

themes: make straight paths for your feet, lest that which is lame be turned out of the way
but let it rather be healed
the call to make straight paths, that the lame be healed
care for the weak believer

schools.summary: The command: 'follow (dioko — pursue, eagerly seek) peace with all men, and holiness (hagiasmon — sanctification, consecration to God), without which no man shall see the Lord' — actively pursuing peaceable relations with everyone (so far as possible, not mere passive avoidance of strife but earnest pursuit of peace) and personal holiness hand in hand; holiness being indispensable, the vision of God reserved for the holy (Matthew 5:8) — no optional extra but a necessity, without sanctification no salvation, none shall see the Lord without it (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Chrysostom).

themes: Follow peace with all men, and holiness, without which no man shall see the Lord
the pursuit of peace and holiness
holiness indispensable to seeing God
no salvation without sanctification

schools.summary: The oversight: 'looking diligently (episkopountes — exercising oversight, watching carefully) lest any man fail of (fall short of, come short of, miss) the grace of God; lest any root of bitterness (Deuteronomy 29:18 — a bitter root, a person or principle producing bitter fruit, apostasy, sin, or a corrupting individual) springing up trouble (annoy, harass) you, and thereby many be defiled (polluted, corrupted)' — the believers to watch over one another lest anyone fall away from or fail to obtain God's grace, and lest a single corrupt person or evil principle, like a bitter root, spring up and cause trouble, spreading its corruption to many if unchecked (one apostate infecting the whole body), hence the need for vigilant mutual oversight (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry).

themes: looking diligently lest any man fail of the grace of God
lest any root of bitterness springing up trouble you, and thereby many be defiled
the watchful diligence against falling and the root of bitterness
vigilant mutual oversight

schools.summary: The double warning: 'lest there be any fornicator (one guilty of sexual immorality), or profane person (bebelos — irreligious, worldly, one who treats sacred things as common), as Esau, who for one morsel of meat (a single meal, the pottage) sold his birthright (prototokia — the rights of the firstborn, including the spiritual blessing)' — two evils to guard against (immorality and profanity, the irreverent godless temper); Esau the type of the 'profane person' (not necessarily a fornicator but profane, holding sacred things cheap, valuing the earthly above the spiritual), who bartered away his sacred privilege for a momentary appetite — the picture of preferring the fleeting and physical to the eternal and holy, a warning against those who would sell their spiritual birthright (their share in Christ) for the world's passing pleasures (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

Cyril: Esau, who for one meal sold his birthright, is the warning to all who would trade their sacred inheritance for fleeting gratification; his profanity lay in counting the holy privilege cheap, preferring the morsel of the moment to the eternal blessing.

themes: lest there be any fornicator, or profane person, as Esau
who for one morsel of meat sold his birthright
the warning against the profane, as Esau
selling the spiritual birthright for the fleeting

schools.summary: The outcome (Genesis 27): 'for ye know how that afterward, when he would have inherited the blessing (sought too late), he was rejected (apedokimasthe — disapproved, refused): for he found no place of repentance, though he sought it carefully with tears.' Clarke (strongly) and Barnes (leaning), with Beza, Doddridge, and Stuart, take 'repentance' (metanoia — change of mind/purpose, not theological contrition) as referring to Isaac, not Esau: Esau 'found no place of repentance' meaning no way to change his father's mind, who having pronounced the blessing could not reverse it (Genesis 27:33). Robertson: Esau a tragic example of a wilful sin that allows no second chance (cf. 6:6; 10:26), pressed as a warning to Christians tempted to give up Christ. All agree the verse does not teach that a penitent sinner cannot be received (Barnes explicitly rejects that misuse) but warns against despising privileges till the loss is irretrievable.

Calvin (strongly), with Wesley and Ellicott's main reading, take 'repentance' as Esau's own: 'he found no room for repentance,' i.e., his late repentance availed nothing to regain the lost blessing — the door was closed, no admission for repentance found (Calvin parallels topos in 8:7, 'no place sought for the second'). Esau's tears were those of a man past hope, not the tears of true conversion or sincere hatred of sin but mere terror at the loss; Calvin insists that whenever a sinner truly sighs for sin God is ready to pardon (Matthew 7:8), but Esau, like the ungodly, did not knock at God's door in faith. Wesley: 'no room for any such repentance as would regain what he had lost'; he sought the birthright-blessing too late.

debates.question: Whose 'repentance' found no place — Isaac's or Esau's?

sides.position: Isaac's: Esau could not change his father's mind to reverse the pronounced blessing
Esau's own: his late repentance found no room to regain the forfeited blessing

themes: when he would have inherited the blessing, he was rejected
for he found no place of repentance, though he sought it carefully with tears
Esau rejected, finding no place of repentance
the crux of whose repentance

schools.summary: The description of Sinai: 'for ye are not come unto the mount that might be touched (a material, tangible mountain, Sinai), and that burned with fire, nor unto blackness (gloom), and darkness, and tempest (storm, whirlwind)' — the readers not come, as Israel did, to the physical Mount Sinai ablaze with fire at the giving of the law (Exodus 19), with its awful, terrifying manifestations (the scene of terror and dread); the writer recalling Sinai's terrors (vv.18-21) precisely to contrast them with the gracious Mount Zion (vv.22-24) to which believers have come — the old covenant coming in terror, the new in grace (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Chrysostom, Henry).

themes: ye are not come unto the mount that might be touched, and that burned with fire
nor unto blackness, and darkness, and tempest
the contrast: not come to the terrors of Sinai
the old covenant in terror

schools.summary: The continuation of the Sinai scene: 'and the sound of a trumpet (the loud trumpet-blast, Exodus 19:16,19), and the voice of words (the spoken words of God, the Decalogue, the articulate voice of God uttering the commandments); which voice they that heard intreated (begged) that the word should not be spoken to them any more' — the terrifying trumpet and the very voice of God speaking the law adding to the dread, so awful that the people, overwhelmed, pleaded that God would not continue to speak directly to them (Exodus 20:19), unable to endure the immediate voice of God (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry).

themes: the sound of a trumpet, and the voice of words
which voice they that heard intreated that the word should not be spoken to them any more
the fearful trumpet and voice at Sinai
the people overwhelmed by God's voice

schools.summary: The reason for the people's terror: '(for they could not endure (bear) that which was commanded' — the command so severe they could not bear it, even the conditions of approach terrifying; 'and if so much as a beast touch the mountain, it shall be stoned, or thrust through with a dart' (Exodus 19:12-13) — the mountain so holy and forbidden that even an animal straying onto it must be killed (not even touched by hand), the absolute barrier marking God's unapproachable holiness under the law; this dreadful exclusion measuring the distance the law kept between God and man, the very opposite of the access believers now have (vv.22-24) — Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott.

themes: they could not endure that which was commanded
if so much as a beast touch the mountain, it shall be stoned, or thrust through with a dart
the unbearable command barring the mountain
the law's dreadful holiness

schools.summary: The climax of the Sinai terrors: 'and so terrible (fearful, dreadful) was the sight (to phantazomenon — the appearance, the spectacle), that Moses said, I exceedingly fear and quake (tremble)' — even Moses, the mediator of the old covenant, overcome with fear and trembling at the awful scene; the saying not recorded in the Exodus account of Sinai (it may draw on Deuteronomy 9:19 or Jewish tradition), but the point standing that even the great lawgiver trembled (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott).

Cyril: if even Moses, the mediator who drew nearest to God, exceedingly feared and quaked, how terrible was the law's giving — and how great, by contrast, the grace and confidence of those who come to Mount Zion (vv.22-24), where the access is one of joy, not dread.

themes: so terrible was the sight, that Moses said, I exceedingly fear and quake
even Moses trembled at the terrible sight
the mediator overcome at Sinai
the contrast with Zion's grace

schools.summary: The turn from Sinai to Zion: 'but ye are come (have come, by faith) unto mount Sion (the symbol of grace and the gospel, over against Sinai of the law), and unto the city of the living God, the heavenly Jerusalem, and to an innumerable company (myriads, tens of thousands) of angels' — believers come not to the terrors of Sinai but to Zion, the city of God, the heavenly Jerusalem (the true, eternal, heavenly dwelling, the home of the redeemed, cf. 11:10,16; Galatians 4:26; Revelation 21), into whose fellowship they have already entered by faith, joining the vast festal assembly of the holy angels (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

Cyril: instead of Sinai's terror, the believer is come to the heavenly Jerusalem and the innumerable festal host of angels — the joyful gathering of the city of God, where grace, not dread, reigns; the access opened by Christ admits believers even now into this heavenly assembly.

themes: ye are come unto mount Sion, and unto the city of the living God, the heavenly Jerusalem
and to an innumerable company of angels
the believers come to Mount Zion, the heavenly Jerusalem
the festal host of angels

schools.summary: The imagery: 'to the general assembly (panegyris — a festal convocation, a joyful gathering, used of the Greek national festivals) and church (ekklesia) of the firstborn (prototokon), which are written (apogegrammenon — enrolled, registered as citizens) in heaven, and to God the Judge of all, and to the spirits of just men made perfect (teteleiomenon — perfected, freed from sin, glorified)' — the joyful assembly of the redeemed; the 'firstborn' read either as all believers (God's privileged people, as Israel was 'firstborn,' Exodus 4:22) or as the most eminent saints/patriarchs (Calvin, Doddridge, Stuart distinguishing them from 'the spirits of just men' = the godly generally); 'God the Judge of all' named solemnly amid the description of privilege to inspire reverence (yet propitious to believers, adverse to their enemies); 'the spirits of just men made perfect' the souls of the righteous now perfected in heaven (Wesley distinguishing NT believers; Ellicott noting not the OT period only) — Barnes, Robertson, Calvin.

themes: the general assembly and church of the firstborn, which are written in heaven
and to God the Judge of all, and to the spirits of just men made perfect
the heavenly assembly: the firstborn, God the Judge, and the perfected spirits
the fellowship of the glorified

schools.summary: The climax of the things believers have come to: 'and to Jesus the mediator (mesites — go-between, reconciler) of the new (neas — fresh, recent) covenant, and to the blood of sprinkling (rhantismou — the sprinkled blood, recalling the old covenant's blood-sprinkling, ch. 9), that speaketh better things than that of Abel' — supremely, believers come to Jesus himself, the mediator of the new covenant, the crown of the heavenly assembly, and to his atoning blood applied to the conscience (ratifying the covenant and cleansing from sin); his blood 'speaking better things than that of Abel' — Abel's blood cried out for vengeance (Genesis 4:10), but Christ's blood speaks of pardon, reconciliation, and mercy (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

Cyril: Christ's blood of sprinkling speaks better than Abel's — where Abel's righteous blood cried from the ground for justice and vengeance, the blood of Jesus pleads for mercy, pardon, and reconciliation; the new covenant's blood is the voice of grace, not of accusation.

themes: Jesus the mediator of the new covenant, and to the blood of sprinkling
that speaketh better things than that of Abel
Jesus the mediator and his blood that speaks better than Abel's
Christ's blood pleading mercy where Abel's cried vengeance

schools.summary: The application of the contrast (argument from lesser to greater): 'see (take heed) that ye refuse not (do not reject, turn a deaf ear to) him that speaketh (the God who speaks in the gospel, or Christ whose blood speaks, v.24). For if they (Israel) escaped not (did not go unpunished) who refused him that spake on earth (God speaking at Sinai through Moses, the earthly mountain), much more shall not we escape, if we turn away from him that speaketh from heaven' — those who rejected the voice at Sinai were punished, so far more will those who reject the heavenly voice of the gospel; the greater the revelation, the greater the guilt of refusing it (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry).

themes: See that ye refuse not him that speaketh
much more shall not we escape, if we turn away from him that speaketh from heaven
the warning not to refuse him who speaks from heaven
the greater revelation the greater accountability

schools.summary: The contrast of past and future: 'whose voice then (at Sinai) shook the earth (the earthquake at the law's giving, Exodus 19:18): but now he hath promised (Haggai 2:6), saying, Yet once more I shake not the earth only, but also heaven' — God's voice once shook the earth at Sinai (a literal trembling of the mountain and ground, the manifestation of his terrible majesty in giving the law), but he promises a future, greater, final shaking embracing heaven as well as earth — the great convulsion that attends the new order and the final consummation, far surpassing the local shaking at Sinai (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: Whose voice then shook the earth
Yet once more I shake not the earth only, but also heaven
the promised final shaking of earth and heaven
Sinai surpassed by the consummation

schools.summary: The interpretation of Haggai's words: 'and this word, Yet once more, signifieth (indicates) the removing (metathesin — removal, change) of those things that are shaken, as of things that are made (created, temporal things), that those things which cannot be shaken may remain' — the very phrase 'once more' implying a final removal of all that can be shaken, after which no further shaking is needed; the things removed being the visible, created, temporal order (including the old covenant system, what is made being shakable and passing), removed precisely so that the unshakable realities (the kingdom of God, the eternal order, the new covenant) may stand and abide forever (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: this word, Yet once more, signifieth the removing of those things that are shaken
that those things which cannot be shaken may remain
the final shaking removes the temporary, leaving the unshakable
the eternal kingdom abiding

schools.summary: The believer's possession and response: 'wherefore we receiving (as a gift, being given) a kingdom which cannot be moved (unshakable, the eternal kingdom of God that remains when all else is shaken), let us have grace (echomen charin — let us have/hold grace, or be thankful, gratitude), whereby we may serve (latreuomen — worship, render sacred service to) God acceptably (in a way well-pleasing to him), with reverence (aidous — modesty, awe) and godly fear (eulabeias — reverent caution, holy fear)' — believers being heirs of the one kingdom that endures the final shaking, the proper response being grateful, acceptable worship and service, rendered not with terror as at Sinai but with reverent awe befitting the holy God (grace not abolishing reverence but deepening it) — Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Chrysostom.

themes: we receiving a kingdom which cannot be moved
let us have grace, whereby we may serve God acceptably with reverence and godly fear
the unshakable kingdom received, calling for reverent service
grace deepening reverence

schools.summary: The reason for reverent fear (Deuteronomy 4:24): 'for our God is a consuming fire (pyr katanaliskon — a fire that consumes, devours)' — the ground of serving with reverence and godly fear being that God himself is a consuming fire, his holiness consuming all that is unholy; the figure expressing God's awful holiness and justice, terrible against sin and the impenitent (the same God of grace also a God of consuming holiness, not to be trifled with); and it is 'our God' — the believer's own God, the God of grace who gives the unshakable kingdom — who is yet a consuming fire, to be approached with awe, for he will consume those who despise his grace (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: For our God is a consuming fire
the ground of reverence: our God is a consuming fire
God's awful holiness consuming the unholy
the God of grace yet a consuming fire
